title: The Burl-Library Grove

features.name: burl-shelves
features.desc-on-touch: ancient eucalyptus trees with burled knots worn smooth by centuries. in the hollows, files rest like they've been shelved there since the forest learned to read. the bark has written dates that don't match any calendar

features.name: carved-pilgrimage
features.desc-on-touch: the trees hold carved records of journeys taken between groves. marks accumulate: someone came here seeking, someone stayed, someone left stories in exchange. the archive treats them as a pilgrimage route

features.name: underwood-sanctuary
features.desc-on-touch: a small shelter built among the lowest roots, where civilisation touches the earth again. files here remember what it was like to live beneath—in contact, without intermediary

features.name: root-reading
features.desc-on-touch: the exposed roots form a text you can trace with your fingers. it's the archive's attempt to read what grows beneath it. the words shift depending on where you place your hand

features.name: tree-memory-ring
features.desc-on-touch: a cross-section of trunk showing rings that record every year the grove held stories. some rings are darker—years of drought or loss. some glow—years when many arrived seeking

inscription: the understory keeps its own library, written in bark and roots; the trees remember what we forgot about living in contact

file.name: Pilgrimage_Routes_Between_Sacred_Groves_Carved_Record.txt
file.note: seekers' marks carved into eucalyptus, a map written by those who sought redemption through return. the archive cannot determine if the trees taught them the way or if the journey wrote itself into th

exits.label: back across the natural bridge to the pooled words
deeper into the understory where the archive cannot follow
back to where words become water again
